title: ADD Command with internal unsigned HTTPS endpoint throws x509: certificate signed by unknown authority error
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

When running podman build, if the docker file has ADD with an unsigned https url with an unsigned certificate, the build container will error out with x509: certificate signed by unknown authority 

In our environment, we do have our own internally signed certs to where this URL is pointing to and they are installed under the containers/certs.d folder. 

Pull works great...just not the ADD command with an internal https endpoint under podman build

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Have build file with an ADD command with unsigned HTTPS url

2. run podman build

3. Should error out

**Describe the results you received:**
Error: error building at STEP "ADD https://<internaly_signed_URL> /dir/other-dir" error reading https://<internaly_signed_URL> : get "https://<internaly_signed_URL> ": x509: certificate signed by unknown authority

**Describe the results you expected:**
Able to build with custom certs in .config/cert.d folder

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
4.1.0
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```

```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```

```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


No

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: Can you volume mount in your certificates?

That would require some pre-processing, correct?  I've recently run into this issue and the best solution I've come up with so far is to do roughly what's mentioned in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/15173 (macOS, non-rootful). It feels kind of clunky, and I'm not sure if I'm misusing `podman system connection default` here, but fortunately, it only needs to be done once after machine init:

```bash
# Ensure we connect as root
podman system connection default podman-machine-default-root
# Copy in the certs
(cd "$certdir" && tar -cf - *.crt | podman machine ssh tar -C /etc/pki/ca-trust/source/anchors/ -xvf -)
# Process the certs
podman machine ssh update-ca-trust extract
# Switch the connection back to non-root
podman system connection default podman-machine-default
```

It would be nice if there were something like the `certs.d` directory that would get automatically processed on machine startup.

Even without that, it would be easier if there were a `podman machine cp` command and some way to specify the user in `podman machine ssh`.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I noticed that podman machine on my mac automatically mounts my `$HOME` (macos) directory into the VM. Why not have podman in podman machine take the home directory to be the same home as the host system on macos so that certs are found without extra work for users?
The issue is the user UIDs is different for every user, I believe and can't easily be handled.  Is there an environment variable that could be set to search the correct location in $HOME?
